definition: The yield curve's recession-prediction record is substantially weaker than the canonical '7/7' claim implies. After Bayesian adjustment for variable lead times (6-24 months), false positives (1966, 1998, 2022-23), and a 25-30% base rate of recession in any 24-month window, the marginal lift is approximately 1.5-2x base rate, not near-certainty.
evidence: 2022-23 inversion (-108bp, deepest since early 1980s) has not produced recession as of March 2026
At least 2-3 false positives depending on classification criteria
25-30% base rate of recession in any 24-month window shrinks marginal predictive lift
pair_2 debate explicitly refuted 'record remains intact' framing as self-contradictory
pair_3 debate agreed challenger_02 'is clearly stronger' on this point
open_questions: What is the actual marginal predictive lift after proper Bayesian adjustment with current data?
Should the framework incorporate global curve synchronization as independent information?
Does the signal retain more value when conditioned on expectations-driven (not term-premium-driven) inversions?
